content.body: The body needs a variety of nutrients for energy, growth, and body maintenance. A balanced diet contains all nutrients in the right proportions.

Seven food components:
1. Carbohydrates — energy (4 kcal/g)
2. Proteins — growth and repair (4 kcal/g)
3. Fats/Lipids — energy store, insulation (9 kcal/g)
4. Vitamins — metabolic processes (small amounts)
5. Minerals — body functions (small amounts)
6. Water — solvent, transport medium
7. Dietary fibre — aids movement of food through gut

content.title: Key Vitamins and Minerals
content.body:
Vitamin C: antiscorbutic (prevents scurvy), fruits
Vitamin D: bone health (rickets), sunlight/fish
Vitamin A: vision, growth, carrots/liver
Iron: haemoglobin (anaemia if deficient), red meat
Calcium: bones/teeth, dairy products
Iodine: thyroid hormone, fish/iodised salt

content.title: Tests for Food
content.body:
Starch: iodine solution → blue-black
Glucose (reducing sugar): Benedict's solution → brick-red precipitate
Protein: Biuret test → purple
Fat: ethanol emulsion test → milky white layer

questions.question: Which nutrient provides the most energy per gram?
questions.explanation: Fats provide 9 kcal/g vs carbohydrates and proteins at 4 kcal/g each

questions.question: Deficiency of Vitamin C causes
questions.explanation: Vitamin C deficiency causes scurvy: bleeding gums, poor wound healing

questions.question: Iodine solution tests for
questions.explanation: Iodine turns blue-black in presence of starch

questions.question: Benedict's test gives brick-red precipitate with
questions.explanation: Benedict's reagent detects reducing sugars (glucose, maltose) by forming brick-red Cu2O precipitate

questions.question: Protein is needed in the diet for
questions.explanation: Proteins are structural (muscle, collagen) and functional (enzymes, antibodies, hormones)

questions.question: Dietary fibre prevents
questions.explanation: Fibre adds bulk to food, stimulates peristalsis, prevents constipation and reduces bowel cancer risk

questions.question: Biuret test purple colour indicates presence of
questions.explanation: Biuret reagent (NaOH + CuSO4) turns purple/violet in presence of peptide bonds (proteins)

questions.question: Calcium deficiency in children causes
questions.explanation: Both calcium and Vitamin D deficiency cause rickets (soft, malformed bones) in children

questions.question: Iodine deficiency causes
questions.explanation: Iodine is needed to make thyroid hormones; deficiency causes thyroid to enlarge (goitre)

questions.question: Emulsion test (ethanol test) detects
questions.explanation: Dissolve food in ethanol, add water: fat produces milky white emulsion

questions.question: What is the basic unit of life?
questions.explanation: The cell is the basic structural and functional unit of all living organisms.

questions.question: Which organelle is responsible for energy production in cells?
questions.explanation: Mitochondria carry out aerobic respiration to produce ATP — the cell's energy currency. Hence called the 'powerhouse of the cell'.

questions.question: What is photosynthesis?
options: The process by which plants make food using sunlight
The breakdown of glucose for energy
The absorption of minerals from soil
questions.answer: The process by which plants make food using sunlight
questions.explanation: Photosynthesis: 6CO₂ + 6H₂O + light energy → C₆H₁₂O₆ + 6O₂. Plants convert light energy to chemical energy stored in glucose.

questions.question: What is osmosis?
options: Movement of water from high to low water potential through a semi-permeable membrane
questions.answer: Movement of water from high to low water potential through a semi-permeable membrane
questions.explanation: Osmosis is the passive movement of water molecules through a semi-permeable membrane from a region of higher water potential to lower.

questions.question: What molecule carries genetic information?
questions.explanation: DNA carries the genetic code in sequences of bases (A, T, G, C) that determine the structure of proteins.

questions.question: What is the function of chloroplasts?
questions.explanation: Chloroplasts contain chlorophyll and are the site of photosynthesis, capturing light energy to make glucose.

questions.question: In genetics, what is a dominant allele?
options: One that is expressed even when only one copy is present
One that is only expressed when two copies are present
questions.answer: One that is expressed even when only one copy is present
questions.explanation: A dominant allele is expressed in the phenotype regardless of whether it is heterozygous or homozygous.

questions.question: What is diffusion?
options: Movement of particles from high to low concentration without energy
questions.answer: Movement of particles from high to low concentration without energy
questions.explanation: Diffusion is the passive movement of particles down a concentration gradient — no energy (ATP) is required.

questions.question: Which blood component carries oxygen?
questions.explanation: Red blood cells contain haemoglobin, which binds to oxygen in the lungs and releases it in body tissues.

questions.question: What is the role of the nucleus?
options: Controls cell activities and contains DNA
questions.answer: Controls cell activities and contains DNA
questions.explanation: The nucleus is the control centre of the cell, containing DNA and directing all cellular activities including protein synthesis.

content.body: Digestion breaks large, insoluble food molecules into small, soluble ones that can be absorbed into the blood. It involves mechanical digestion (physical breakdown) and chemical digestion (enzymes).

Digestive organs and their roles:
Mouth: teeth chew (mechanical), salivary amylase digests starch → maltose
Stomach: churning (mechanical), pepsin digests protein, HCl kills bacteria, pH~2
Small intestine (duodenum+ileum): enzymes from pancreas, bile from liver; absorption
Large intestine: water reabsorption, bacterial action
Rectum/Anus: storage and egestion of faeces

content.title: Digestive Enzymes
content.body:
Amylase: starch → maltose (mouth, pancreas)
Protease/Pepsin: protein → amino acids (stomach)
Lipase: fat → fatty acids + glycerol (small intestine)
Maltase: maltose → glucose (small intestine)

Bile: made in liver, stored in gall bladder
→ Emulsifies fats (breaks into droplets) for lipase

content.title: Absorption in Ileum
content.body:
Villi: finger-like projections increase surface area
Microvilli: further increase area
Villi features: thin wall (1 cell thick), rich blood supply (capillaries), lacteal (for fats)
Glucose and amino acids: absorbed into blood (portal vein → liver)
Fatty acids: absorbed into lacteals (lymphatic system)

questions.question: Salivary amylase breaks down
questions.explanation: Amylase in saliva begins starch digestion in the mouth

questions.question: The stomach produces HCl to
options: kill bacteria and provide acidic pH for pepsin
questions.answer: kill bacteria and provide acidic pH for pepsin
questions.explanation: HCl creates pH~2, kills pathogens and activates pepsin (protein-digesting enzyme)

questions.question: Bile is made in the
questions.explanation: Bile is produced in the liver, stored in the gall bladder, released into duodenum

questions.question: Bile emulsifies fats, meaning it
options: breaks large fat droplets into smaller ones for lipase
questions.answer: breaks large fat droplets into smaller ones for lipase
questions.explanation: Emulsification increases surface area for lipase enzyme to digest fat

questions.question: Villi in the small intestine
questions.explanation: Villi (and microvilli) massively increase surface area of ileum for efficient nutrient absorption

questions.question: Glucose and amino acids absorbed in ileum go to
questions.explanation: Nutrients from digestion travel in hepatic portal vein to liver for processing

questions.question: Fatty acids are absorbed into
questions.explanation: Fats are insoluble — absorbed into lymphatic lacteals in villi, eventually entering blood via thoracic duct

questions.question: Water is mainly reabsorbed in
questions.explanation: Large intestine (colon) reabsorbs most water from undigested material, forming solid faeces

questions.question: Enzyme produced by pancreas that digests fat =
questions.explanation: Pancreatic lipase breaks fats into fatty acids and glycerol

questions.question: Peristalsis is
options: wave-like muscle contractions moving food along digestive tract
questions.answer: wave-like muscle contractions moving food along digestive tract
questions.explanation: Circular and longitudinal muscles contract alternately, creating waves that push food through the gut

questions.question: What is the basic unit of life?
questions.explanation: The cell is the basic structural and functional unit of all living organisms.

questions.question: Which organelle is responsible for energy production in cells?
questions.explanation: Mitochondria carry out aerobic respiration to produce ATP — the cell's energy currency. Hence called the 'powerhouse of the cell'.

questions.question: What is photosynthesis?
options: The process by which plants make food using sunlight
The breakdown of glucose for energy
The absorption of minerals from soil
questions.answer: The process by which plants make food using sunlight
questions.explanation: Photosynthesis: 6CO₂ + 6H₂O + light energy → C₆H₁₂O₆ + 6O₂. Plants convert light energy to chemical energy stored in glucose.

questions.question: What is osmosis?
options: Movement of water from high to low water potential through a semi-permeable membrane
questions.answer: Movement of water from high to low water potential through a semi-permeable membrane
questions.explanation: Osmosis is the passive movement of water molecules through a semi-permeable membrane from a region of higher water potential to lower.

questions.explanation: DNA carries the genetic code in sequences of bases (A, T, G, C) that determine the structure of proteins.

questions.question: What is the function of chloroplasts?
questions.explanation: Chloroplasts contain chlorophyll and are the site of photosynthesis, capturing light energy to make glucose.

questions.question: In genetics, what is a dominant allele?
options: One that is expressed even when only one copy is present
One that is only expressed when two copies are present
questions.answer: One that is expressed even when only one copy is present
questions.explanation: A dominant allele is expressed in the phenotype regardless of whether it is heterozygous or homozygous.

questions.question: What is diffusion?
options: Movement of particles from high to low concentration without energy
questions.answer: Movement of particles from high to low concentration without energy
questions.explanation: Diffusion is the passive movement of particles down a concentration gradient — no energy (ATP) is required.

questions.question: Which blood component carries oxygen?
questions.explanation: Red blood cells contain haemoglobin, which binds to oxygen in the lungs and releases it in body tissues.

questions.question: What is the role of the nucleus?
options: Controls cell activities and contains DNA
questions.answer: Controls cell activities and contains DNA
questions.explanation: The nucleus is the control centre of the cell, containing DNA and directing all cellular activities including protein synthesis.

content.body:
Photosynthesis equation:
6CO₂ + 6H₂O + light energy → C₆H₁₂O₆ + 6O₂

Two stages:
1. Light-dependent reactions (thylakoid membranes):
   Water splits (photolysis) → O₂ released
   ATP and NADPH produced

2. Light-independent (Calvin cycle, stroma):
   CO₂ fixed using ATP and NADPH
   Glucose (G3P) produced

content.title: Chloroplast Structure
content.body:
Chlorophyll in thylakoid membranes absorbs light
Grana: stacks of thylakoids (light reactions)
Stroma: fluid matrix (Calvin cycle)

Chlorophyll absorbs red and blue light best
Reflects green light (why plants look green)

content.title: Factors Affecting Rate
content.body:
Light intensity: rate ↑ with more light (up to a limit)
CO₂ concentration: rate ↑ with more CO₂
Temperature: rate ↑ to optimum (~25°C), then ↓ (enzymes denature)
Water availability: stomata close in drought reducing CO₂ uptake

questions.question: Photosynthesis converts light energy to
questions.explanation: Photosynthesis: light energy → chemical energy (glucose). Energy is stored in C-H bonds

questions.question: Oxygen is released in photosynthesis from
questions.explanation: O₂ comes from H₂O: 2H₂O → 4H⁺ + 4e⁻ + O₂ in light reactions

questions.question: The Calvin cycle occurs in
questions.explanation: Calvin cycle (light-independent reactions) takes place in the stroma using CO₂, ATP, and NADPH

questions.question: Chlorophyll appears green because
options: it reflects green light (absorbs red and blue)
questions.answer: it reflects green light (absorbs red and blue)
questions.explanation: Chlorophyll absorbs red (660nm) and blue (430nm) light for photosynthesis; green is reflected to our eyes

questions.question: Rate of photosynthesis is limited by CO₂ when
options: CO₂ is scarce but light and temperature are adequate
questions.answer: CO₂ is scarce but light and temperature are adequate
questions.explanation: When other factors are adequate, low CO₂ becomes the limiting factor

questions.question: Above optimum temperature, photosynthesis rate falls because
questions.explanation: High temperature denatures enzyme active sites, including rubisco which fixes CO₂

questions.question: In light-dependent reactions, ATP is produced for use in
questions.explanation: ATP from light reactions powers the Calvin cycle where CO₂ is reduced to G3P (glucose precursor)

questions.question: A variegated leaf (part green, part white) can photosynthesize only in
questions.explanation: Only cells with chlorophyll (green parts) can photosynthesize; white areas lack chloroplasts

questions.question: Increasing light intensity increases rate of photosynthesis until
questions.explanation: At high light intensity another factor (CO₂ concentration or temperature) becomes the new limiting factor

questions.question: The raw materials for photosynthesis are
questions.explanation: Photosynthesis inputs: CO₂ (from air through stomata) + H₂O (from roots) + light energy

questions.explanation: The cell is the basic structural and functional unit of all living organisms.

content.body: In this lesson, we explore advanced aspects of Nutrition. Build on what you have already learned and apply your knowledge to more challenging problems.
Learning Objectives

By the end of this lesson you should be able to: (1) Apply Nutrition concepts to complex problems. (2) Use appropriate formulas and methods. (3) Interpret results in context. (4) Connect this topic to related areas of biology.
Core Theory

This section of Nutrition builds on foundational knowledge. In biology, advanced problem-solving requires not just knowing formulas but understanding when and how to apply them. Study each worked example carefully.

content.title: Worked Example 4.1
content.body: Consider a more complex problem on Nutrition. Approach: (1) Read and understand the problem. (2) Identify all given information. (3) Determine which concepts apply. (4) Solve systematically, showing all steps. (5) Check your answer is reasonable.

content.title: Worked Example 4.2
content.body: Another type of problem you may encounter: Questions may test your ability to combine multiple concepts within Nutrition. Practice identifying the type of problem and the appropriate method before attempting to solve.
Common examination questions on Nutrition include: definitions and explanations, numerical calculations, diagram interpretation, and analysis of given data. Make sure you can handle all types.
Summary and Review
You have now covered all lessons in Nutrition. Review the key concepts from each lesson, practice past paper questions, and ensure you can apply your knowledge confidently. Good luck in your studies!

questions.question: What is the basic unit of life?
questions.explanation: The cell is the basic structural and functional unit of all living organisms.

questions.question: Which organelle is responsible for energy production in cells?
questions.explanation: Mitochondria carry out aerobic respiration to produce ATP — the cell's energy currency. Hence called the 'powerhouse of the cell'.

questions.question: What is photosynthesis?
options: The process by which plants make food using sunlight
The breakdown of glucose for energy
The absorption of minerals from soil
questions.answer: The process by which plants make food using sunlight
questions.explanation: Photosynthesis: 6CO₂ + 6H₂O + light energy → C₆H₁₂O₆ + 6O₂. Plants convert light energy to chemical energy stored in glucose.

questions.question: What is osmosis?
options: Movement of water from high to low water potential through a semi-permeable membrane
questions.answer: Movement of water from high to low water potential through a semi-permeable membrane
questions.explanation: Osmosis is the passive movement of water molecules through a semi-permeable membrane from a region of higher water potential to lower.

questions.question: What molecule carries genetic information?
questions.explanation: DNA carries the genetic code in sequences of bases (A, T, G, C) that determine the structure of proteins.

questions.question: What is the function of chloroplasts?
questions.explanation: Chloroplasts contain chlorophyll and are the site of photosynthesis, capturing light energy to make glucose.

questions.question: In genetics, what is a dominant allele?
options: One that is expressed even when only one copy is present
One that is only expressed when two copies are present
questions.answer: One that is expressed even when only one copy is present
questions.explanation: A dominant allele is expressed in the phenotype regardless of whether it is heterozygous or homozygous.

questions.question: What is diffusion?
options: Movement of particles from high to low concentration without energy
questions.answer: Movement of particles from high to low concentration without energy
questions.explanation: Diffusion is the passive movement of particles down a concentration gradient — no energy (ATP) is required.

questions.question: Which blood component carries oxygen?
questions.explanation: Red blood cells contain haemoglobin, which binds to oxygen in the lungs and releases it in body tissues.

questions.question: What is the role of the nucleus?
options: Controls cell activities and contains DNA
questions.answer: Controls cell activities and contains DNA
questions.explanation: The nucleus is the control centre of the cell, containing DNA and directing all cellular activities including protein synthesis.

questions.question: What is respiration?
options: The process of releasing energy from glucose in cells
questions.answer: The process of releasing energy from glucose in cells
questions.explanation: Cellular respiration: C₆H₁₂O₆ + 6O₂ → 6CO₂ + 6H₂O + ATP. It releases energy from glucose for cell use.

questions.question: What is natural selection?
options: Organisms with favourable traits survive and reproduce more
All organisms evolving at the same rate
questions.answer: Organisms with favourable traits survive and reproduce more
questions.explanation: Natural selection is Darwin's mechanism: individuals with traits better suited to the environment survive and pass on those traits.

questions.question: What organ filters blood in humans?
questions.explanation: The kidneys filter blood to remove waste products (urea, excess water, salts), producing urine.

questions.question: What is the function of stomata in leaves?
questions.explanation: Stomata are pores in leaves that allow CO₂ in and O₂ out during photosynthesis, and water vapour out during transpiration.

questions.question: Which part of the neurone carries impulses away from the cell body?
questions.explanation: The axon is the long fibre that carries nerve impulses away from the cell body to other neurons or effectors.

questions.question: What is the function of enzymes?
options: Biological catalysts that speed up reactions
questions.answer: Biological catalysts that speed up reactions
questions.explanation: Enzymes are protein catalysts that lower activation energy and speed up biochemical reactions without being consumed.

questions.question: What gas do plants absorb during photosynthesis?
questions.explanation: Plants absorb CO₂ from the air through stomata during photosynthesis, converting it to glucose using light energy.

questions.question: What is a food chain?
options: A sequence showing how energy passes from one organism to another
A list of foods animals eat
questions.answer: A sequence showing how energy passes from one organism to another
questions.explanation: A food chain shows the transfer of energy from producers (plants) through consumers. Example: Grass → Rabbit → Fox.

questions.question: What is the function of the cell membrane?
options: Controls what enters and leaves the cell (selectively permeable)
questions.answer: Controls what enters and leaves the cell (selectively permeable)
questions.explanation: The cell membrane is selectively permeable, controlling the passage of substances in and out of the cell.

questions.question: What is mitosis?
options: Cell division producing two genetically identical daughter cells
questions.answer: Cell division producing two genetically identical daughter cells
questions.explanation: Mitosis is cell division for growth and repair, producing two daughter cells with identical chromosomes to the parent cell (diploid).

One that is only expressed when two copies are present
questions.answer: One that is expressed even when only one copy is present
questions.explanation: A dominant allele is expressed in the phenotype regardless of whether it is heterozygous or homozygous.

questions.question: What is diffusion?
options: Movement of particles from high to low concentration without energy
questions.answer: Movement of particles from high to low concentration without energy
questions.explanation: Diffusion is the passive movement of particles down a concentration gradient — no energy (ATP) is required.

questions.question: Which blood component carries oxygen?
questions.explanation: Red blood cells contain haemoglobin, which binds to oxygen in the lungs and releases it in body tissues.

questions.question: What is the role of the nucleus?
options: Controls cell activities and contains DNA
questions.answer: Controls cell activities and contains DNA
questions.explanation: The nucleus is the control centre of the cell, containing DNA and directing all cellular activities including protein synthesis.

questions.question: What is respiration?
options: The process of releasing energy from glucose in cells
questions.answer: The process of releasing energy from glucose in cells
questions.explanation: Cellular respiration: C₆H₁₂O₆ + 6O₂ → 6CO₂ + 6H₂O + ATP. It releases energy from glucose for cell use.

questions.question: What is natural selection?
options: Organisms with favourable traits survive and reproduce more
All organisms evolving at the same rate
questions.answer: Organisms with favourable traits survive and reproduce more
questions.explanation: Natural selection is Darwin's mechanism: individuals with traits better suited to the environment survive and pass on those traits.

questions.question: What organ filters blood in humans?
questions.explanation: The kidneys filter blood to remove waste products (urea, excess water, salts), producing urine.

questions.question: What is the function of stomata in leaves?
questions.explanation: Stomata are pores in leaves that allow CO₂ in and O₂ out during photosynthesis, and water vapour out during transpiration.

questions.question: Which part of the neurone carries impulses away from the cell body?
questions.explanation: The axon is the long fibre that carries nerve impulses away from the cell body to other neurons or effectors.

questions.question: What is the function of enzymes?
options: Biological catalysts that speed up reactions
questions.answer: Biological catalysts that speed up reactions
questions.explanation: Enzymes are protein catalysts that lower activation energy and speed up biochemical reactions without being consumed.

questions.question: What gas do plants absorb during photosynthesis?
questions.explanation: Plants absorb CO₂ from the air through stomata during photosynthesis, converting it to glucose using light energy.

questions.question: What is a food chain?
options: A sequence showing how energy passes from one organism to another
A list of foods animals eat
questions.answer: A sequence showing how energy passes from one organism to another
questions.explanation: A food chain shows the transfer of energy from producers (plants) through consumers. Example: Grass → Rabbit → Fox.

questions.question: What is the function of the cell membrane?
options: Controls what enters and leaves the cell (selectively permeable)
questions.answer: Controls what enters and leaves the cell (selectively permeable)
questions.explanation: The cell membrane is selectively permeable, controlling the passage of substances in and out of the cell.

questions.question: What is mitosis?
options: Cell division producing two genetically identical daughter cells
questions.answer: Cell division producing two genetically identical daughter cells
questions.explanation: Mitosis is cell division for growth and repair, producing two daughter cells with identical chromosomes to the parent cell (diploid).
